front: What does 42 U.S.C. § 1983 create, and against whom?
back: A private civil action for damages or injunctive relief against any person who, under color of state law, deprives someone of a federal constitutional right.

front: Under § 1983, deprivation of WHAT is actionable — rights or mere injuries?
back: Rights. § 1983 protects rights, privileges, or immunities secured by the Constitution and federal law — not mere injuries.

front: What does 42 U.S.C. § 1988(b) authorize in a § 1983 action?
back: The court, in its discretion, may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party.

front: Under § 1988, who ultimately bears a prevailing civil-rights plaintiff's attorney's fees when the defendant is a government?
back: The public fisc — the fees are taxpayer-funded.

front: What does 18 U.S.C. § 242 make a federal crime?
back: Willfully depriving a person of constitutional or federal rights under color of law — the criminal analog to § 1983.

front: Why are 18 U.S.C. § 242 indictments reserved in practice for the most egregious violations?
back: Because the government must prove the officer acted willfully (specific intent), a steep burden proven beyond a reasonable doubt.

front: DISTINCTION: How does § 1983 (civil) differ from 18 U.S.C. § 242 (criminal) on burden and intent?
back: § 1983 = civil damages, proven by a preponderance, with no willfulness element. § 242 = criminal prosecution, proven beyond a reasonable doubt, requiring a willful (specific-intent) deprivation.

front: Which Supreme Court case construed the "willfully" requirement of 18 U.S.C. § 242?
back: Screws v. United States, 325 U.S. 91 (1945).

front: What did Monroe v. Pape (1961) hold about the reach of "under color of" state law?
back: "Under color of" law reaches an officer's misuse of state-conferred authority — even conduct that state law itself forbids.

front: Monroe v. Pape (quoting Classic): "{{Misuse of power}}, possessed by virtue of state law and made possible only because the wrongdoer is clothed with the authority of state law, is action taken 'under color of' state law."
back: Misuse of power

front: Under Monroe v. Pape, can an officer who acts illegally still be acting "under color of" state law?
back: Yes. The badge — authority possessed by virtue of state law — is what makes the deprivation actionable, even when the conduct violates state law.

front: Which Monroe v. Pape holding was later overruled, and by what case?
back: Monroe's holding that municipalities are not "persons" under § 1983 was overruled by Monell v. Department of Social Services (1978).

front: What did Monell v. Department of Social Services (1978) hold about municipalities under § 1983?
back: Municipalities ARE "persons" suable under § 1983, but only when an official policy or custom causes the constitutional injury.
cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

front: Can a municipality be held liable under § 1983 on a respondeat superior theory (Monell)?
back: No. "[A] municipality cannot be held liable under § 1983 on a respondeat superior theory" — it is not liable merely for employing a tortfeasor.
cite: Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. at 691

front: Under Monell, what must a plaintiff identify to reach the municipality itself under § 1983?
back: A policy, custom, or final-policymaker decision whose execution inflicts the injury — not merely a single bad-acting employee.
cite: Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. at 694

front: PITFALL: Why does suing the city "because it employs the wrongdoer" fail under § 1983?
back: Monell forecloses respondeat superior — you must point to a policy or custom that caused the injury, not just a bad employee.
cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

front: How did Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982) reformulate qualified immunity — objective or subjective?
back: As a purely objective test: officials are shielded unless their conduct violated clearly established law; it abandoned the old subjective good-faith/malice prong.

front: Harlow: officials "are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate {{clearly established}} statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known."
back: clearly established

front: Under Harlow's objective QI test, what is the real question — whether the officer meant well, or something else?
back: Not whether the officer meant well; whether the law was clearly established at the time the officer acted.

front: Why did Harlow drop qualified immunity's subjective good-faith prong?
back: Because the subjective component was incompatible with resolving insubstantial claims on summary judgment; an objective standard permits early disposition.

front: What two-step qualified-immunity sequence did Saucier v. Katz (2001) establish?
back: (1) Do the facts, viewed most favorably to the plaintiff, show a constitutional violation? and (2) was that right clearly established at the time, judged at a specific level?

front: What is the FIRST prong of the Saucier qualified-immunity analysis?
back: Taken in the light most favorable to the plaintiff, do the facts alleged show the officer's conduct violated a constitutional right?

front: What is the SECOND prong of the Saucier qualified-immunity analysis, and at what level is it judged?
back: Whether the right was clearly established at the time — and it must be considered at a specific (not general) level.

front: What did Pearson v. Callahan (2009) change about the Saucier two-step sequence?
back: It made the sequence discretionary — courts may decide either prong first; the Saucier order "should no longer be regarded as mandatory."

front: Pearson v. Callahan: "while the sequence set forth there is often appropriate, it should no longer be regarded as {{mandatory}}."
back: mandatory

front: After Pearson, what can happen to the underlying constitutional question when a court grants QI on "not clearly established"?
back: The court can grant immunity without ever deciding whether a right was violated, leaving the underlying constitutional question unresolved.

front: DISTINCTION: How do Saucier and Pearson differ on the ORDER of the QI prongs?
back: Saucier made the two-step order mandatory (violation first); Pearson made the sequence discretionary, letting courts decide either prong first.

front: DISTINCTION: How does qualified immunity differ from the Leon good-faith exception?
back: Qualified immunity is a civil defense to § 1983 damages. The Leon good-faith exception is a separate criminal-side doctrine about whether evidence is suppressed under the exclusionary rule.

front: PITFALL: The maxim "no good-faith exception to a warrantless search — it's objective reasonableness" refers to which doctrine, and which doctrine should it NOT be merged with?
back: It refers to Harlow's objective qualified-immunity standard; do not merge it with Leon's good-faith exception to the exclusionary rule.

front: PITFALL: Does qualified immunity affect whether evidence is suppressed in a criminal trial?
back: No. QI never determines whether evidence comes in or out of a criminal trial — it only limits civil damages.

front: Under Graham v. Connor (1989), by what standard are excessive-force § 1983 seizure claims judged?
back: The Fourth Amendment's "objective reasonableness" standard — subjective "malice" and "sadism" have no proper place in the inquiry.

front: How do the QI side (Harlow) and the merits side (Graham) relate on the role of subjective intent?
back: They march in lockstep: objective reasonableness governs both, and the officer's subjective good or bad faith is irrelevant on each side.

front: PITFALL: Post-Harlow/Graham, does a pure good-faith motive save objectively unreasonable conduct (or a bad motive defeat objectively reasonable conduct)?
back: No. A good-faith motive does not save objectively unreasonable conduct, and a bad motive does not defeat objectively reasonable conduct.

front: At what level of generality must "clearly established" law be defined for the QI second prong, and why does it matter?
back: At a specific, particularized level. Defining the right too generally (e.g., "the right to be free from excessive force") rarely overcomes QI; concrete, articulated facts do.

front: What weight does Wright v. City of Euclid (6th Cir. 2020) carry on the QI standard?
back: Persuasive only — one circuit, illustrative. The binding spine is Harlow/Saucier/Pearson (SCOTUS); do not anchor the QI standard to Wright.
cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d 852 (6th Cir. 2020)

front: What did the Sixth Circuit do with qualified immunity in Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive)?
back: It reversed the grant of QI on Fourth Amendment claims — excessive force, false arrest, extended detention, and malicious prosecution — finding a jury could find clearly-established violations.
cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d 852 (6th Cir. 2020)

front: What concrete, date-anchored conduct did Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive) hold was clearly established as excessive force?
back: Drawing a weapon on a suspect who was not fleeing or posing a safety risk, and tasering a suspect who was not actively resisting arrest (clearly established as of Nov. 4, 2016).
cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d at 868

front: What does Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive) illustrate about whether QI is an absolute shield?
back: QI is a meaningful but not absolute shield — where a reasonable jury could find a clearly-established Fourth Amendment violation, immunity is denied.
cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d at 868, 874

front: Which case made § 1983 operative against individual officers, holding that misuse of state-conferred authority is action "under color of" law and requiring no exhaustion of state remedies?
back: Monroe v. Pape, 365 U.S. 167 (1961).

front: Which case holds that a municipality is liable under § 1983 only for injuries caused by an official policy or custom, with no respondeat superior?
back: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978).
cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

front: DISTINCTION: What is the principal § 1983 hurdle for suing an individual officer versus suing the agency?
back: Suing the individual officer: overcome qualified immunity (clearly-established law). Suing the agency: prove a Monell policy or custom (no respondeat superior).

front: Under Ashcroft v. al-Kidd (2011), when is a right "clearly established" for qualified immunity?
back: Only when existing precedent placed the question "beyond debate" — clearly established law is not defined at a high level of generality.

front: al-Kidd: "We do not require a case directly on point, but existing precedent must have placed the statutory or constitutional question {{beyond debate}}."
back: beyond debate

front: What is the dispositive "clearly established" question under Mullenix v. Luna (2015) (per curiam)?
back: Whether the violative nature of the particular conduct is clearly established — the inquiry must be particularized to the specific context, not framed generally.

front: Mullenix v. Luna (per curiam): "The dispositive question is 'whether the {{violative nature of particular conduct}} is clearly established.'"
back: violative nature of particular conduct

front: For the "clearly established" standard, what does District of Columbia v. Wesby (2018) require of the legal principle?
back: It must be "settled law" — a sufficiently clear foundation in then-existing precedent that places the question "beyond debate." (Cite Wesby for the clearly-established standard, not its probable-cause holding.)
cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. 48, 63 (2018)

front: Wesby (clearly-established standard): "To be clearly established, a legal principle must have a sufficiently clear foundation in then-existing precedent. The rule must be '{{settled law}}.'"
back: settled law
cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. at 63

front: Under Rivas-Villegas v. Cortesluna (2021) (per curiam), what must a § 1983 plaintiff identify to show a clearly-established violation?
back: A case that put the officer on notice that his specific conduct was unlawful — the inquiry is undertaken in light of the specific context, not as a broad general proposition.

front: Rivas-Villegas v. Cortesluna (per curiam): the plaintiff "must identify a case that put [the officer] on notice that his {{specific conduct}} was unlawful."
back: specific conduct

front: What does City of Tahlequah v. Bond (2021) (per curiam) warn courts not to do, and whom does QI protect?
back: Courts must not define clearly established law "at too high a level of generality"; qualified immunity protects "all but the plainly incompetent or those who knowingly violate the law."
cite: City of Tahlequah v. Bond, 595 U.S. 9, 11-12 (2021) (per curiam)

front: Tahlequah v. Bond (per curiam): qualified immunity protects "'all but the {{plainly incompetent}} or those who knowingly violate the law.'"
back: plainly incompetent
cite: City of Tahlequah v. Bond, 595 U.S. at 11-12

front: What does Hope v. Pelzer (2002) establish as the counterweight to the QI specificity requirement?
back: A right can be clearly established without a factually identical case — in an "obvious case," officials have fair warning that their conduct is unlawful even in novel factual circumstances.

front: Hope v. Pelzer: "officials can still be on notice that their conduct violates established law even in {{novel factual circumstances}}."
back: novel factual circumstances

front: DISTINCTION: Read together, how do al-Kidd and Hope v. Pelzer define what defeats qualified immunity?
back: Either particularized precedent placing the question "beyond debate" (al-Kidd) OR an "obvious case" giving fair warning without a case on point (Hope) will defeat QI.

front: What did Connick v. Thompson (2011) hold about Monell failure-to-train liability and a single Brady violation?
back: A single Brady violation cannot support failure-to-train liability; a pattern of similar constitutional violations is "ordinarily necessary" to show deliberate indifference.

front: Connick v. Thompson: "A pattern of similar constitutional violations by untrained employees is '{{ordinarily necessary}}' to demonstrate deliberate indifference for purposes of failure to train."
back: ordinarily necessary

front: PITFALL: Why does treating a single bad incident as Monell failure-to-train fail under Connick v. Thompson?
back: Connick requires a pattern of similar violations to show deliberate indifference; one incident almost never suffices to reach the municipality on a failure-to-train theory.

front: What did Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents (1971) recognize, and how does it relate to § 1983?
back: An implied damages remedy against federal officers for Fourth Amendment violations — the federal-officer analog to § 1983 (which reaches only action under color of state law).
cite: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)

front: How did Egbert v. Boule (2022) cabin the Bivens remedy?
back: If there is even a single reason to pause before applying Bivens in a new context, a court may not recognize a Bivens remedy — the question often resolves to whether Congress might be better equipped to create the remedy.

front: Egbert v. Boule: "If there is even a single 'reason to pause before applying Bivens in a new context,' a court may {{not}} recognize a Bivens remedy."
back: not

front: DISTINCTION: § 1983 versus Bivens — which actors does each reach?
back: § 1983 reaches state and local actors (action under color of state law). Bivens is the analog for federal officers — but it is now sharply cabined (Egbert) and nearly foreclosed outside its original Fourth Amendment context.

front: PITFALL: Can you sue a federal officer (DEA, FBI, CBP) under § 1983?
back: No — § 1983 reaches only action under color of state law. A federal officer is reached, if at all, via Bivens, now nearly foreclosed outside its original Fourth Amendment context (Egbert).

front: When does Heck v. Humphrey (1994) bar a § 1983 damages action?
back: When a judgment for the plaintiff would necessarily imply the invalidity of an outstanding conviction or sentence — the suit is not cognizable unless that conviction has already been invalidated (the favorable-termination bar).

front: Under Heck v. Humphrey, how must the underlying conviction be invalidated before the § 1983 action can proceed?
back: The plaintiff must prove the conviction or sentence was reversed on direct appeal, expunged by executive order, declared invalid by an authorized state tribunal, or called into question by a federal writ of habeas corpus.

front: PITFALL: May a defendant convicted on challenged evidence file a parallel § 1983 suit attacking that same search while the conviction stands?
back: Generally no. Heck bars the suit if success would necessarily imply the invalidity of the still-standing conviction.
